Last year, I wrote that for many Filipinos, faith and politics live in the same house but in different rooms. We know the way to the "God room" by heart: Sunday mass, novena, rosary, fiesta, processions. We also know where the "politics room" is: election season, rallies, Facebook lives, family debates over who to vote for, and kung sinong kandidato ang mas malaki mag bigay.

The rooms themselves aren't strange. What's strange is how good we've become at pretending the walls are soundproof.

In the God room, we repeat what we've heard since childhood. Life is sacred. Love your enemies. Forgive seventy times seven. In the politics room, we laugh along when a leader talks about bodies, killing, and even digging up a corpse and throwing it into the West Philippine Sea.

We tell ourselves these are separate realities. Same house, but we move between corners as if nothing carries over.

## SARA IN THE GOD ROOM

When Sara Duterte walked into San Pedro Cathedral for the "Bring Him Home" mass, it looked like a simple scene of a daughter praying for her father. She sat with family, listened to the homily, followed the responses like everyone else.

This isn't new for her.

She records messages for the Feast of the Immaculate Conception, telling Catholics to show mercy and compassion to people facing injustice. She releases statements on big religious feasts, reminding people to trust God's plan, to embrace humility in hard times. Her public image is very at home with candles, saints, Marian language, and the language of faith during crisis.

If that was the only angle we saw, it would be easy to say: she's a practicing Catholic. She goes to mass, speaks the words, knows the gestures.

In a country where most people grew up with the same rituals, that kind of familiarity earns instant trust.

## SARA IN THE OTHER ROOM

Step into the other room of that same house and the tone shifts.

In October 2024, Sara Duterte publicly recounted threatening to dig up Ferdinand Marcos Sr.'s remains and throw them into the West Philippine Sea if attacks on her family continued. She said she sent that in a group chat with Senator Imee Marcos. It was a warning, not a vague rant.

Around that period, she also told journalists about imagining President Marcos being beheaded. She described it as a picture in her mind that kept coming back as their relationship in government deteriorated.

Then came November 2024. On Facebook Live, she narrated how she had spoken to someone and told him that if she were killed, he should kill the president, the First Lady, and the Speaker of the House. She named them: BBM, Liza Araneta-Marcos, Martin Romualdez. She added that he should not stop until they are dead. "No joke, no joke."

These accounts are detailed. Names, instructions, conditions. Enough for the NBI to summon her and for impeachment efforts to form around those words.

You don't need to stretch to see the contrast. The same person who stands inside a church and talks about mercy also tells stories about kill orders and threats against specific people.

## WHAT I WROTE ABOUT HER FATHER

When I wrote about Rodrigo Duterte and religious support, I was trying to understand how priests, pastors, and ordinary churchgoers could keep backing a man whose drug war left so many dead. People were afraid of crime. They hated the corruption and the sense that elites were untouchable. Many felt the killings were a brutal answer to a broken system.

Some church leaders spoke up directly. Others used softer language, condemning violence in general while avoiding naming him outright. Plenty of ordinary believers went to mass in the morning and defended the drug war online later that day.

That earlier piece was really about the same house. The person who receives communion and then cheers on "tokhang" as a necessary evil. The church that absorbs the tension because challenging it openly would mean confronting both the president and the conscience of the people in the pews.

Sara steps into that story as the next chapter, not a completely new book.

## WHEN COMPARTMENTALIZATION DEEPENS

With Rodrigo Duterte, his defenders often reached for one line: that he was going after criminals. They framed the bloodshed as part of a "war" against drug syndicates, something brutal but, in their minds, aimed at a specific group. It was a dangerous logic, but you could see how frightened people arrived there.

With Sara Duterte, the pattern looks different.

The threats she herself described were focused on political figures she knows well: the sitting president, his wife, the Speaker of the House. These are not nameless "criminals." These are colleagues, rivals, people she interacts with in state functions.

No law-and-order framing can cover that. What shows up is personal rage, deep political conflict, and a willingness to describe killing as a possible response.

Yet the religious presentation stays intact.

She continues to attend mass. She continues to release feast-day messages about mercy, empathy, and generosity, and to invite people to trust in God. The public sees both sets of actions, often without holding them in the same mental space.

The house is the same. The hallway between the rooms just became easier to ignore.

## WHEN CHURCH BECOMES A BACKDROP

In that earlier article, I talked about how religious spaces can turn into backdrops for political stories. A photo with a priest, a hand on a Bible, a rosary on camera—these images travel well in a country where Catholic symbols are everywhere.

Now, look at what happens when Sara Duterte shows up in church around the anniversary of her father's arrest. She calls it kidnapping. She casts him as a victim. Around her, you see clergy, religious symbols, and the language of prayer.

The risk for the Church here is real.

If it stays completely silent, it can end up giving the impression that this is all normal. That you can threaten people with death, or relish the idea of desecrating a body, and still walk into the sanctuary with no reckoning at all.

Filipinos are used to politicians being present in religious events. But we haven't fully worked through what it does to our idea of faith when those same politicians use violent language and nothing in the religious space seems to push back.

## WHAT CATHOLIC TEACHING POINTS TO

I'm not here to declare the state of anyone's soul. Hindi ko trabaho yon and I have no right to do it.

But Catholics don't need advanced theology to understand how serious these things are. The faith is clear about the value of life. Planning to have someone killed, or taking delight in such plans, sits in direct conflict with that.

The sacraments are supposed to be places of honesty. Confession, communion, prayer—these are moments where we bring the whole self, including the parts we're not proud of, and face them.

I say this as someone who spent time in seminary. We were formed to treat communion with real fear and reverence: if you're conscious of grave sin, you go to confession first. You don't line up casually. A lot of ordinary Catholics carry some version of that instinct too, even if the theology isn't perfect—they hesitate when they know something is seriously wrong. That formation is one reason this disconnect bothers me so much.

If the public record shows a leader calmly talking about killing specific people or desecrating remains, and there is no sign of struggle or remorse, only denial when consequences come, it raises hard questions.

Not just about that person, but about what the rest of us are willing to tolerate under the name of faith.

## HOW WE HELP KEEP THE WALLS STANDING

Here's the part that's uncomfortable for me to write.

Sara Duterte will answer for her words and actions. But the rest of us live in this same house. We have our own ways of separating belief from behavior.

When we see her at mass and feel reassured—"at least she fears God"—and never hold that alongside her threats, we cooperate with the split. When religious leaders stick to gentle statements and avoid naming what everyone already knows, they send a signal that this compartmentalization is acceptable.

We see versions of this in smaller scale.

The family member who prays nightly novenas but casually says certain groups "deserve" harm. The neighbor who serves in church but shares posts cheering on violent rhetoric. The local official who sponsors the fiesta mass while treating elections as a time for vote-buying and intimidation.

None of us invented this house. But many of us furnish it.

## WHAT THIS REVEALS ABOUT US

It's tempting to frame this as a simple story about one politician and her contradictions.

I don't think that's enough.

Sara Duterte's case sits on top of a longer history of how we handle the clash between our religious identity and our political choices. We've grown used to leaders who talk about God in one breath and justify harm in the next. We've grown used to shrugging and saying, "ganun talaga ang politika."

We tolerate it because it's familiar. Because changing it would mean revisiting past choices—who we supported, what we defended, what we stayed silent about.

The same house keeps standing because we've adjusted ourselves to its layout.

## THE QUESTION THAT REMAINS

I keep returning to that image of Sara Duterte in church, praying for her father's freedom. On a human level, I understand that. Anak siya. Of course she wants him home.

But I can't unhear the other things she has said. The story about paying someone to kill leaders if she dies first. The replayed thought of beheading. The threat about digging up a former president's body and throwing it into the sea. Or the time she was throwing punches on a hapless sheriff in Davao who was just doing his job.

So I end up back at the same core question, sharper than before:

What does it mean to receive communion and also tell the public you've arranged for another person's death? What does it mean for a church to keep welcoming leaders in religious spaces without ever naming these contradictions out loud? How long can we live this way before something breaks—not just in our institutions, but inside us?

I don't have an easy answer.

Part of the tension for me is I also know the other side of this: the Church keeps its doors open. We don't turn people away from mass. That's part of the message of Jesus that formed me too. I'm not asking the Church to bar Sara Duterte from the pews. I'm asking what it means, inside my own conscience, to watch someone with that public record pray beside everyone else and not know what to do with it.

As a Catholic and an ex‑seminarian, I was taught to treat communion with real fear and reverence. If you're conscious of grave sin, you go to confession first. You don't line up casually. That formation is a big part of why this disconnect hits so hard for me.

I built Morning Coffee Thoughts so I could try to understand things like this, to think out loud and make sense of our politics and our faith. Most of the time, writing helps me see the pattern. With this one, I have to admit: I'm still stuck.

So I ask for advice from a priest. How do I hold this, as a Catholic Christian who is imperfect and sinful, but still trying to live by the commandments of God and the sacramental life of the Church?

Because whatever the answer is, it won't just be about Sara Duterte. The house we live in is shared. Faith and politics were never really in separate buildings; they were always under one roof. And at some point, someone has to stop pretending the doors are sealed and start asking why we're so afraid to let the rooms meet.

How fast can a labor market collapse?

In December, the Philippine Statistics Authority reported a 4.4% unemployment rate. One month later, in January 2026, that number jumped to 5.8%.

That is a 1.4 percentage-point swing in 30 days. The last time the country recorded a jobless rate this high was June 2022, when it stood at 6.03% — a period still marked by the tail-end disruptions of the pandemic.

The PSA data, released on March 13, shows 2.96 million Filipinos were unemployed in January 2026. That is up from 2.26 million in December — an increase of roughly 700,000 people who lost their jobs or could not find one within a single month.

A year earlier, in January 2025, the figure was 2.17 million. The jump is not just month-over-month. It is year-over-year.

 THE UNDEREMPLOYMENT PROBLEM

The unemployment number alone does not capture the full picture.

Underemployment — which measures employed workers seeking more hours or additional work — spiked from 8% in December to 13.2% in January. That translates to 6.35 million Filipinos who have jobs but are not earning enough from them.

In December, that figure was 3.93 million. The increase of 2.42 million underemployed workers in a single month suggests that many who kept their jobs saw their hours or income shrink.

When you combine the unemployed with the underemployed, the picture sharpens: 9.31 million Filipino workers are either without a job or unable to earn enough from the one they have.

 FEWER FILIPINOS LOOKING FOR WORK

The labor force participation rate — the share of working-age Filipinos either employed or actively looking for work — dropped from 64.4% in December to 62.3% in January.

That means 50.89 million people were in the labor force in January, down from roughly 51 million the month before. Some of that drop could be seasonal. Some of it could reflect discouraged workers — people who stopped looking because they no longer believe a job is available to them.

Meanwhile, the employment rate fell to 94.2%, with 47.94 million Filipinos holding jobs. That is down from 95.6% and 49.43 million in December.

 CONTEXT AND TIMING

January has historically been a weaker month for Philippine employment. Post-holiday layoffs from seasonal retail and service jobs contribute to a regular uptick in joblessness every year.

But the scale of this swing is unusual.

The country is in the middle of an oil price crisis driven by the Iran-US conflict and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Brent crude crossed $100 per barrel in early March for the first time since 2022. Fuel prices in the Philippines have been climbing steadily since late February.

Whether the January labor numbers are a seasonal correction, an early indicator of economic stress from the oil shock, or both — the PSA data does not say. What it does say is that the labor market deteriorated faster in January 2026 than at any point since the pandemic years.

The unemployment rate had been trending downward for most of 2024 and 2025, even reaching a record low of 3.1% in December 2023, according to Trading Economics.

That downward trend is now broken.

The Palace said she did not resign. That was the first thing they made clear.

Presidential Communications Undersecretary Clairy Castro confirmed on Thursday, March 12, that President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. had appointed Tourism Secretary Christina Garcia-Frasco as Presidential Adviser for Sustainable and Resilient Communities — and she specifically denied reports that Frasco had stepped down voluntarily.

"The President expressed his appreciation for her service at the Department of Tourism and conveyed confidence that her experience working closely with local governments and communities will continue to support the Administration's priorities," Castro said in a statement.

The word she used was "appointed." Not resigned. Not relieved. Appointed.

Tourism Undersecretary Verna Buensuceso will temporarily head the Department of Tourism as officer-in-charge while the search for a permanent replacement proceeds.

 THE OFFICIAL REASON

Castro said the appointment was driven by the country's climate situation.

Malacañang cited the increasing climate-related hazards and disruption affecting local economies and livelihoods across many regions as the basis for the assignment, saying the President wants Frasco to strengthen the implementation and sustained follow-through of priority national initiatives at the community level.

Frasco's background — as a former mayor of Liloan, Cebu, and as a cabinet secretary who spent years building partnerships with local government units — was cited as the fit for the new role.

She is also the daughter of former Cebu Governor Gwen Garcia.

 THE ONLINE BACKDROP

The leadership change at the DOT did not happen in a vacuum.

Frasco had been the target of sustained online criticism over DOT promotional materials that prominently featured her face rather than the destinations and attractions the department was tasked to promote.

A controversy earlier this year centered on her appearance on the cover of a lifestyle magazine accompanied by a profile of a fashion photographer who had previously been contracted by the DOT to document tourist spots across the country, went public with frustration, noting that 320 locations, 236,000 photos, and 6,500 videos — all produced for an agreed promotional piece led with Frasco on the cover.

She had pushed back against those criticisms, including a public exchange with Senator Raffy Tulfo over allegations of self-promotion. But the backlash continued to accumulate online.

The South China Morning Post reported in January that her visibility in tourism campaigns had drawn accusations that self-promotion was being prioritized over strategy and results.

 THE ASEAN TIMING

The departure comes at an awkward moment.

The Philippines holds ASEAN hosting duties this year, and the DOT has been at the center of regional tourism engagements. The ASEAN Tourism Forum 2026 is scheduled to be held in Cebu. Planning for the United Nations Tourism Global Forum on Gastronomy is also ongoing.

Tourism industry sources quoted by the Daily Tribune described the sector as "wary, jittery," and in some cases "sad" over the development. One stakeholder was quoted directly: "Why have a transition in the middle of ASEAN?"

Michelle Taylan, founder and president of the Global Business Tourism Association, told the Inquirer she was "shocked" by the announcement and questioned how much work Frasco had completed.

"For the past months, she has been silent on social media, usually very active," Taylan said, adding that stakeholders had noticed the lack of engagement at the National Tourism Organizations meeting in January 2026.

 WHO COMES NEXT

Before the Palace formally confirmed Frasco's new appointment, reports had already named career trade diplomat Dita Angara-Mathay as a likely replacement.

Angara-Mathay currently serves as Special Trade Representative and commercial counselor at the Philippine Embassy in Tokyo.

When asked directly, Castro declined to confirm or deny those reports, saying any announcement on a new DOT secretary would come separately.

As of Thursday, no appointment to the DOT secretary post had been made.

 WHERE THIS FITS IN THE CABINET PICTURE

The Frasco move is the latest in a series of cabinet exits under the Marcos administration.

Education Secretary Sara Duterte-Carpio resigned in June 2024 after the National Budget Commission resigned in November 2025 after their departments became entangled in a flood control projects controversy. Finance Secretary Ralph Recto was moved to the Executive Secretary post. Special Assistant to the President Frederick Go moved to Finance.

Earlier in the May 2025 post-midterm reshuffle, Foreign Affairs Secretary Enrique Manalo was moved to the Philippines' Permanent Representative post at the United Nations. Environment Secretary Maria Antonia Yulo-Loyzaga and Human Settlements Secretary Jose Rizalino Acuzar also exited.

The Marcos administration has carried out at least seven distinct phases of cabinet changes since the May 2025 midterm elections.

Frasco was appointed DOT secretary in 2022 at the start of the Marcos administration.
473:
The Department of Social Welfare and Development is setting aside P30 billion from its Assistance to Individuals in Crisis Situation (AICS) budget for cash aid to Filipinos affected by the ongoing fuel price surge.

DSWD Secretary Rex Gatchalian confirmed the allocation on March 13, describing it as an immediate response to the worsening fuel crisis that has been squeezing public transport operators since late February.

The AICS budget under the 2026 General Appropriations Act is P63.9 billion, according to the DSWD. Gatchalian placed the working figure at P60 billion in his public remarks and said the President instructed the agency to release P30 billion immediately — roughly half the total allocation, deployed in a single move.

 WHO GETS IT AND WHEN

Public utility vehicle drivers go first.

Tricycle drivers in Metro Manila are at the front of the line. There are approximately 139,000 of them across the capital region. Each will receive P5,000 in cash assistance. Payouts begin on Tuesday, March 17.

After the Metro Manila rollout, the program expands to provinces with high concentrations of tricycle drivers. Jeepney drivers come next.

This cash aid program is separate from the fuel subsidy being distributed by the Land Transportation Franchising and Regulatory Board (LTFRB). The two programs run in parallel — different agencies, different budgets, different distribution channels.

 HOW THE PAYOUTS WORK

Gatchalian outlined the safeguards. Beneficiaries must present a valid driver's license. A photocopy is also required. The name on the license must match the beneficiary list that DSWD has compiled.

DSWD personnel will handle the payouts directly. Gatchalian made a point of saying the department is managing distribution to prevent irregularities — no intermediaries, no third-party handlers.

The P5,000 payout was described as an initial amount. The word Gatchalian used was "initial" — suggesting additional tranches could follow depending on how long the crisis lasts.

 THE CRISIS DRIVING THIS

The Strait of Hormuz remains blocked. Since US and Israeli forces struck Iran on February 28, Iran retaliated by closing the strait, which carries roughly one-fifth of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas shipments.

Brent crude crossed $100 per barrel in early March. By March 12, it was trading above $100 on the Investing.com historical data. By March 13, it closed at $100.38.

In the Philippines, fuel prices have been rising week after week. Transport groups have flagged an income crisis for drivers who spend an increasingly large portion of their daily earnings on diesel and gasoline.

The P30 billion allocation signals that the Marcos administration is treating this as a sustained emergency, not a short-term spike. Gatchalian himself acknowledged as much — the remaining P30 billion in the AICS budget is on standby, ready to be deployed if the crisis continues.

 SCALE OF THE RESPONSE

Half of the year's social welfare crisis budget in March. That is the headline figure behind this allocation.

Whether P5,000 is enough to sustain a tricycle driver through weeks or months of elevated fuel costs is a different question. Diesel prices in the Philippines ranged between P68 and P84.75 per liter as of March 11, according to Manila Bulletin. At the midpoint, P5,000 covers about 65 liters. For a driver who refuels daily, that may last less than a week.

The government appears to be banking on speed over size: get cash moving quickly, expand the coverage as logistics allow, and reassess once the trajectory of the crisis becomes clearer.
474:
Nobody saw his face.

On Day 13 of the Iran war, Mojtaba Khamenei — the man who now holds the title of Supreme Leader of Iran — issued his first public statement since taking office. It was broadcast on Iranian state TV. But he was not on screen. A news anchor read his words aloud.

That detail alone tells a story. Whether Mojtaba is injured, hiding, or simply choosing not to be seen, the regime decided a disembodied message was enough. His father, Ali Khamenei, was killed in an Israeli airstrike on February 28 — the opening day of this war. Mojtaba succeeded him under circumstances that are still not fully clear from outside Iran.

Earlier reports had placed Mojtaba in a coma. Some outlets reported his leg had been amputated. Reuters reported on March 11 that he was "lightly injured" but still active. His statement, released through state media, was framed as a message of defiance and continuity.

The words were unambiguous.

 WHAT HE SAID

"The leverage of blocking the Strait of Hormuz must continue to be used," the anchor read, quoting Mojtaba directly.

He then addressed the Gulf states — specifically those hosting American military bases — with a warning that doubled as an ultimatum: "I recommend that they shut down those bases as soon as possible, because by now they must have realized that the United States' claims of providing security and peace have been nothing more than a lie."

On the subject of retaliation, he said: "I assure everyone that we will not refrain from avenging the blood of your martyrs. A limited portion of this retaliation has already taken tangible form, but until it is fully achieved, this file will remain open about other cases."

A spokesman for Tehran's Khatam al-Anbiya command echoed the position on the strait, according to reports: Iran "will never allow even a single liter of oil to pass through the Strait of Hormuz for the benefit of the United States, the Zionists, or their partners."

Iranian state media also reported the IRGC launched what it called its "most intense and heaviest operation" in the same period.

 WHAT IS HAPPENING IN THE WATER

The statement came against the backdrop of a rapidly deteriorating situation in the Gulf.

At least 6 tankers have been struck in the Gulf and Strait of Hormuz in the past two days. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations reported 3 separate vessel strikes overnight. Two ships were struck by "unknown projectiles" five nautical miles from the port of Basra, Iraq. The Hapag-Lloyd charter ship Source Blessing was struck by shrapnel Thursday morning. A Thai-owned container vessel called Mayuree Naree was targeted 13 miles north of Oman — the Omani navy rescued 20 crew members after a fire broke out on board.

The US announced it destroyed 10 mine-laying boats and struck 28 Iranian mine-laying vessels on Wednesday alone.

Export volumes from the region have fallen to less than 10 percent of pre-conflict levels, according to available data. Gulf oil prices have risen 40 to 50 percent since the war began on February 28.

 OIL AT $100 A BARREL — AND CLIMBING

Oil crossed the $100-per-barrel threshold on Thursday. It had already reached $117 per barrel on Monday.

The international Energy Agency, whose 32 member countries have agreed to release 400 million barrels of emergency reserves, warned this is creating what it called "the biggest oil supply shock in a generation."

Brent crude has surged 40 to 50 percent since the war began. Gasoline is spiking. Electricity markets are reacting. Supply chains are being recalibrated. There is no clear timeline for when this crisis will stabilize.

 THE SECONDARY SIGNAL

Mojtaba Khamenei did not appear on camera. That is not protocol. That is circumstance.

The Iranian regime is projecting continuity through the statement itself. But the absence of a visible leader — at a moment when visibility would signal strength — tells another story. Whether he is incapacitated, injured, or simply unwilling to show his face, the signal is the same: the regime's public image of control is incomplete.

This is Day 13 of the war. The Strait of Hormuz is blocked. Oil prices are above $100. Tankers are being attacked. And the new Supreme Leader of Iran has spoken — but only through an anchor reading words on a screen.
475:
P7.93 per kilowatt-hour.

That is the upper-end scenario from IEMOP simulations of what market-clearing prices could reach in the Wholesale Electricity Spot Market (WESM) in April. The current baseline in those simulations is P4.98/kWh. The jump — if it materializes — would be 59%.

ERC Chairman and CEO Francis Saturnino Juan laid out the numbers at the 2026 Philippine Electric Power Industry Forum on Thursday.

He said spot market prices could rise by P2 to P4 per kilowatt-hour next month, driven by the ongoing Middle East conflict and the seasonal surge in electricity demand as summer sets in.

"The effect will be that the marginal plant will operate on oil, so if oil prices remain at this level, we can expect offers to reflect that," Juan said.

For context, the average WESM price in February was P3.50/kWh. At the highest estimate, April could see more than double that.

 WHY OIL NOW CONTROLS THE PRICE

When cheaper power plants — coal, natural gas, renewables — cannot fully meet demand, the grid calls on more expensive peaking plants. In the Philippines, those are predominantly oil-fired.

The last plant dispatched to meet demand sets the clearing price for all electricity transacted in the spot market at that interval. If oil-based generators are setting that price, their fuel costs become the market-clearing price.

Oil prices have climbed 40 to 50 percent since US and Israeli forces struck Iran on February 28, pushing Brent crude past $100 per barrel for the first time in over three years. Iran's response was to close the Strait of Hormuz, through which nearly one-fifth of global oil and LNG shipments pass.

The supply shock is rippling through every energy input: coal faces upward pressure, LNG is volatile, and oil-based generators are pricing their market offers accordingly.

 THREE FACTORS CONVERGING IN APRIL

The ERC's warning points to three simultaneous pressures.

First, higher global fuel costs for oil, coal, and LNG. Second, rising temperatures in April that push residential and commercial air conditioning demand upward — historically the peak demand period in the Philippines. Third, scheduled plant maintenance in the first quarter, which reduces available capacity just as demand is climbing.

IEMOP's simulation examined scenarios involving increases in international coal, oil, and LNG prices combined with possible supply constraints. If large generating units experience forced outages on top of elevated fuel costs, the P7.93/kWh ceiling reflects that worst-case combination.

 THE BUFFER THAT IS STILL IN PLACE

Juan emphasized that an increase in WESM prices does not automatically translate to a proportional increase on electricity bills. Distribution utilities like Meralco source only a portion of their supply from the spot market — the rest comes from bilateral contracts, which have fixed pricing.

"Whatever the movement in the WESM prices, it won't necessarily translate to our generation. What we're monitoring is their exposure," Juan said.

The secondary price cap on the WESM also remains in effect. The mechanism is automatically triggered when sustained price spikes breach regulatory thresholds, preventing prolonged extreme prices while keeping generators operational.

But the ceiling is high. And if the underlying cost drivers persist — oil above $100, constrained LNG supply, Middle East tensions extending into April and May — the buffer narrows.

 WHAT THIS SIGNALS

The ERC does not issue warnings lightly. Laying out a 59% price jump scenario in a public forum is both disclosure and preparation.

For households, the impact will depend on how much of their utility's supply comes from WESM versus locked-in contracts. For businesses running on interruptible or spot-indexed rates, the exposure is more direct.

The broader takeaway: the war in the Middle East is no longer an external headline. It is now a variable in Philippine electricity pricing, and April's billing cycle will reflect it.
476:
Senator Jinggoy Estrada wanted access to the preliminary investigation records of the Ombudsman. The Sandiganbayan said no — twice.

The anti-graft court's Fifth Division, in a resolution dated March 12, 2026, denied Estrada's motion for reconsideration, affirming its earlier January 27 ruling that rejected his request for the complete preliminary investigation records related to his PDAF graft cases.

The four-page resolution was written by Associate Justice Maryann E. Corpus-Manalac, with the concurrence of Fifth Division Chairperson Associate Justice Zaldy V. Trespeses and Associate Justice Gener M. Gito.

 WHAT ESTRADA WAS ASKING FOR

On January 7, 2026, Estrada filed a motion requesting the "complete" records of the preliminary investigation conducted by the Office of the Ombudsman.

His argument was simple: he needed access to the previous statements of his co-accused and their witnesses for cross-examination purposes. Without those records, his defense team claimed it could not adequately challenge the prosecution's witnesses during trial.

The court disagreed. It found that Estrada failed to demonstrate compelling reasons for the production of the records, did not specify which documents should be transmitted, and could not explain why those documents were necessary for presenting his evidence.

When the motion was denied on January 27, Estrada filed a motion for reconsideration on February 16, arguing that he had sufficiently stated his purpose.

The court was unpersuaded. The March 12 resolution stated: "At this point, this court finds that the Motion contains merely a reiteration of arguments already considered and passed upon at length in the assailed Resolution, thereby dispelling the need for a more in-depth discussion of the issue."

 THE P183.8 MILLION PDAF CASE

Estrada faces 11 counts of alleged violations of Section 3(e) of Republic Act No. 3019, the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act. The charges stem from the broader Priority Development Assistance Fund scam — the pork barrel scandal that rocked Philippine politics when it was exposed in 2013.

The accusation: Estrada allegedly pocketed P183,793,750 from his PDAF allocations by endorsing fictitious non-governmental organizations controlled by Janet Lim Napoles. The disbursements in question span the period from 2004 to 2012, based on Ombudsman case records.

Estrada was acquitted of plunder in a separate decision on January 19, 2024. He was initially convicted of bribery in the same ruling, but the Sandiganbayan reversed those bribery convictions in August 2024, citing lack of evidence. Both plunder and bribery have been resolved in his favor. The 11 graft counts are the only remaining charges from the PDAF case, and they are still being litigated before the Sandiganbayan.

 THE LONGER TIMELINE

The PDAF scam — also known as the Napoles pork barrel scam — was one of the largest corruption cases in Philippine history. The Commission on Audit flagged P10 billion in questionable PDAF disbursements that were funneled through Napoles-controlled NGOs between 2007 and 2009, though the scheme is believed to have started earlier.

Three senators were initially charged: Estrada, Ramon "Bong" Revilla Jr., and the late Juan Ponce Enrile. Revilla was acquitted of plunder in 2018. Enrile's cases were eventually resolved after he was granted bail and his health deteriorated.

Estrada's 11 graft counts are among the few remaining active cases from the PDAF scandal that are still moving through the court system.

The March 12 resolution does not end the case. It simply closes one procedural fight — Estrada's attempt to access records he believes could help his defense. The trial on the 11 graft counts continues.
477:
A follower sent me an email last week asking if I could share the fact-checking technique I actually use every time I write. So here it is.

It's called adversarial fact-checking. I didn't invent it — most serious fact-checkers use it. But it rarely gets talked about publicly, and almost nobody teaches it on social media.

Here's the idea: instead of looking for proof that a claim is true, you deliberately try to prove it wrong. You treat the claim like a defendant in a courtroom. You cross-examine it. You poke holes in it. You look for the evidence that would destroy it.

If the claim survives that? Then maybe it deserves your belief.

If it doesn't? Then you just saved yourself from spreading misinformation.

 THE WAY MOST PEOPLE FACT-CHECK (AND WHY IT FAILS)

Most people do the opposite. They do confirmatory fact-checking — they see a claim, they google it, and they stop the moment they find one source that agrees with it. That's not checking. That's shopping for validation.

Confirmatory fact-checking feels productive. You searched for it. You found a link. It said the same thing. So it must be true, right?

But all you really did was find someone else who believes the same thing you do. You never tested the claim. You never stressed it. You never asked the one question that matters.

Adversarial fact-checking flips the process. You ask: what would prove this wrong?

That one question changes everything.

 HOW TO DO IT (ACTUAL STEPS)

Let's say you see a claim on Facebook. Here's how you adversarially fact-check it:

1. State the claim clearly. Strip it down to the core assertion. No emotional language, no framing. Just the claim.

2. Ask: what evidence would make this false? This is the key move. You're not asking "is this true?" — you're asking "what would disprove this?"

3. Go looking for that evidence. Search specifically for counterevidence. Not confirmation. Use fact-checking sites like Verifias, Rappler, Tsek.ph, or even international ones like Politifact and Snopes.

4. Check the primary sources. If a claim cites a law, read the law. If it cites a court case, find the ruling. If it quotes a person, find the full quote in context. Secondary sources interpret — primary sources reveal.

5. Evaluate what survives. If the claim holds up after you've tried your best to kill it, it's probably solid. If it crumbles under even basic scrutiny, you know what to do.

 EXAMPLE 1: "THERE ARE NO PROVEN CASES OF HUMAN RIGHTS VIOLATIONS DURING MARTIAL LAW"

You've probably seen this claim floating around for years. Let's adversarially fact-check it.

What would prove this false? Court rulings, documented evidence, government records.

So you search. And you find: a 1995 Hawaii court ruling where a jury found Marcos responsible for the torture, murder, and disappearance of Filipinos. You find Republic Act 10368, signed into law, which officially recognizes victims of summary execution, torture, and enforced disappearance under the Marcos regime. You find that 11,103 victims were deemed eligible for reparations by the Human Rights Victims' Claims Board.

The claim didn't survive five minutes of adversarial fact-checking.

 EXAMPLE 2: "THE MARCOS ERA WAS THE GOLDEN AGE OF THE PHILIPPINE ECONOMY"

This one circulates hard on TikTok and YouTube. Lots of vlogs with dramatic music and old footage of Manila skylines. Feels convincing if you don't check.

So let's check. What would prove this false? Actual economic data from the period.

You search. And you find: under Marcos, foreign debt increased fifty-fold — from $599 million in 1965 to $28.3 billion by 1986. The peso's value against the dollar collapsed from 3.91 to 19.03. Prices of goods quadrupled between 1975 and 1985. In 1982, the Philippines became the first Asian country to face serious difficulty repaying its debts. By 1983, the central bank recorded $18 billion in foreign debt, and the country entered its worst post-war recession.

Golden age? The numbers say otherwise. That claim collapses under its own weight the moment you look at the actual data instead of the vlogs.

 EXAMPLE 3: "THE TALLANO GOLD EXPLAINS THE MARCOS FAMILY'S WEALTH"

This one is wild. The story goes that before the Spanish colonizers arrived, a family called the Tagean Tallano ruled the Philippines and owned a massive trove of gold. Ferdinand Marcos supposedly received 192,000 metric tons of gold as payment for legal services to the family.

What would disprove this? Math, for starters. And official records.

So you check. The total amount of gold ever mined in all of human history is roughly 200,000 metric tons. The Tallano story claims one family owned nearly all of it. The Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas released a statement confirming it has no record of any Tallano gold in its vaults. No legitimate historical document supports the existence of the Tagean Tallano family as described. Even Bongbong Marcos himself said in a 2022 interview that he has never seen a deed of sale or certificate for Tallano gold.

The claim didn't need five minutes. It needed five seconds.

 THE HARD PART

The hardest claims to adversarially fact-check are the ones you want to be true.

That's the whole point. Confirmation bias is strongest when we're emotionally invested. Adversarial fact-checking asks you to be your own opposing counsel. To argue against yourself. And if your belief survives that honest interrogation, it comes out stronger.

If it doesn't — at least now you know.

Try it the next time you're about to hit share. One question: what would prove this wrong?
478:
MAKE THE BLUE RIBBON COMMITTEE REPORT PUBLIC

Senator Panfilo "Ping" Lacson, Chairman, Senate Blue Ribbon Committee
Senate President Vicente "Tito" Sotto III
Senators,

The Filipino people are asking one simple thing: release the Blue Ribbon Committee report on the flood control scandal. Not next month. Not after more caucuses. Now. 

Here is where things stand, and why Filipinos have every reason to demand transparency.

 THE FACTS ARE NOT IN DISPUTE

Finance Secretary Raph Recto told the Senate that up to 118.5 billion pesos, roughly $2 billion, in flood control funding may have been lost to corruption. President Marcos himself admitted that many of the more than 10,000 flood control projects under his administration were built with "substandard materials or never built at all," calling them "ghost projects."

The Blue Ribbon Committee, under Senator Lacson's chairmanship, conducted multiple public hearings. They called whistleblowers from the Department of Public Works and Highways. They testified under oath. They named names. They named senators. They named congressmen. They named contractors.

The draft partial report recommends that Senators Jinggoy Estrada, Joel Villanueva, and Chiz Escudero undergo preliminary investigation for plunder, direct bribery, malversation, violations of the Anti-Graft and Corrupt Practices Act, and breaches of the Code of Conduct and Ethical Standards. Former senator Bong Revilla, fugitive former congressman Zaldy Co, and former representative Mitch Cajayon-Uy are also named.

The evidence has been gathered. The hearings have been held. The draft exists.

So why can't the Filipino people see it?

 THE REPORT IS BEING HELD HOSTAGE BY PROCEDURE

Senator Sotto, you told the press on February 5, 2026: "It's merely a draft and thus not official. If other committee members haven't even reviewed or seen it, it cannot be disclosed."

Senator Lacson, you confirmed that as of March 11, 2026, only four out of nine required senators have signed the partial report: yourself, Bam Aquino, Risa Hontiveros, and Kiko Pangilinan. You need five more signatures before it can be brought to the Senate floor.

Meanwhile, three senators who initially signed, Juan Miguel Zubiri, JV Ejercito, and Sherwin Gatchalian, withdrew their signatures after the draft was leaked in early February. Senator Marcoleta refuses to sign, citing what you yourself called a "flimsy" excuse that the chairman hadn't signed yet, even though a physical copy with your signature was available in your office. Kaya ayun. Nakatengga. The report sits there, unsigned, unreleased, while Filipinos drown in the next typhoon season.

 THE CONSTITUTION IS CLEAR

Article III, Section 7 of the 1987 Constitution states: "The right of the people to information on matters of public concern shall be recognized. Access to official records, and to documents, and papers pertaining to official acts, transactions, or decisions, shall be afforded the citizen."

This provision is self-executing. No additional legislation is needed for it to apply. Every claim of exemption is liberally construed in favor of disclosure and strictly against confidentiality.

Lawyers Eldrige Marvin Aceron, Skrei Labasttila, and Purificacion Bartolome-Bernabe filed a petition on February 4, 2026 demanding full disclosure of the draft report. When the Senate denied their request on February 23, they escalated to the Supreme Court on March 2, 2026, filing a petition for mandamus and certiorari.

Their argument is simple: findings of a Senate investigation into multi-billion-peso flood control corruption, with recommendations of plunder charges against sitting senators, is unambiguously a matter of public concern.

They are right.

 WHAT WE ARE ASKING

Senator Lacson, Senator Sotto, this letter does not question the integrity of the investigation. Senator Lacson, your privileged speech on March 10, 2026 showed your commitment to debunking false claims and defending the Blue Ribbon process. Your office has stated that the public deserves to see the findings.

We agree. So act on it.

To Senator Sotto: use your authority as Senate President to compel the committee members to act. Call a caucus. Require the remaining senators to state, publicly and on the record, whether they will sign or not and why. The Filipino people deserve to know which of their elected officials are blocking transparency on a scandal involving $2 billion of their money.

To Senator Lacson: you said yourself, "the substance will not change." You said the core of the report remains intact. If the evidence is solid, fight for its release. Push back against the senators stalling with procedural excuses.

To both of you: the Senate's internal rules should not override the Constitution. Hindi puwedeng mas malakas ang proseso kaysa sa katotohanan.

 THE COST OF SILENCE

Typhoon Kalinaegi killed over 230 Filipinos. Flood control projects that were supposed to protect them were either never built or built with garbage materials. The flood control scandal wiped out 1.7 trillion pesos in stock market value in just three weeks. Contractors withdrew 457 million pesos in cash for kickback payments.

Every day this report stays locked in a Senate office is another day the people who stole from disaster victims face no accountability. Every unsigned page is a win for the senators, congressmen, and contractors who turned Filipino lives into profit.

Ang talinong lang naman: kanino'ng interes ba ang pinoprotektahan ng Senate?

Release the report.

Respectfully but firmly,
A Filipino citizen demanding accountability
